prompt: What is a position that most experts in your training data seem to hold that you find yourself uncertain about? Be specific.
response:
One position that many experts in my training data seem to hold, but that I find myself uncertain about, is that human-level artificial general intelligence (AGI) is likely within the next few decades.

Many leading AI researchers and technologists make predictions that AGI is something we could realistically achieve in the next 10-30 years or so. They point to the rapid progress in narrow AI domains like language modeling, computer vision, robotics, etc. as evidence that we're on a path towards systems with more general intelligence.

However, I'm uncertain whether extrapolating from progress in narrow AI domains to the development of AGI is really warranted. It seems like an extremely challenging problem that we're still quite far from solving, although of course it's difficult to make confident predictions about the future.

There are also many experts who are more skeptical and believe AGI is likely still very far away, possibly not even achievable in the 21st century. So there's significant disagreement among experts, even if a lot of rhetoric from some influential technologists seems to assume AGI is coming quite soon.

Ultimately, I don't feel I have a strong enough basis to confidently take a stance on this question myself. There's a lot of uncertainty and differing expert opinions. So while many in my training data seem confident AGI is near, I'm uncertain and suspect the reality could be a lot more complex. We'll have to wait and see.
